From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

National song of India

This article is about the poem and song. For the song album, see [*Vande Mataram* (album)](/wiki/Vande_Mataram_(album) "Vande Mataram (album)").

"Bande Mataram" redirects here. For other uses, see [Bande Mataram (disambiguation)](/wiki/Bande_Mataram_(disambiguation) "Bande Mataram (disambiguation)").

"**Vande Mātaram**"[[a]](#cite_note-1)[[b]](#cite_note-2) is a poem that was adopted as the national song of the [Republic of India](/wiki/Republic_of_India "Republic of India") in 1950. It was written in [Sanskritised Bengali](/wiki/Sadhu_bhasha "Sadhu bhasha")[[1]](#cite_note-3)[[2]](#cite_note-4) by [Bankim Chandra Chatterjee](/wiki/Bankim_Chandra_Chatterjee "Bankim Chandra Chatterjee") in the 1870s,[[3]](#cite_note-5)[[4]](#cite_note-6) and was first published in 1882 as part of Chatterjee's [Bengali novel](/wiki/Bengali_literature "Bengali literature") *[Anandmath](/wiki/Anandmath "Anandmath")*.[[5]](#cite_note-7)

The poem is an ode to the motherland, it was officially adopted by the Indian National Congress in 1905 to serve as a pan-Indian, nationalist salutation to [Mother India](/wiki/Bharat_Mata "Bharat Mata") during the [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"), culminating in its 1950 designation as the National Song of the Republic of India, sharing equal honour with [India's National Anthem](/wiki/Jana_Gana_Mana "Jana Gana Mana").[[6]](#cite_note-PIB-Anthem1-8)

The first two verses of the song make abstract reference to the "mother" and "[motherland](/wiki/Motherland "Motherland")", without any religious connotation. However, later verses mention [Hindu goddesses](/wiki/Hindu_goddess "Hindu goddess") such as [Durga](/wiki/Durga "Durga").[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[8]](#cite_note-10) The references to Hindu gods became a point of contention in 1937, after which the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"), on suggestion of [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") and [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore"), adopted the two stanzas of the song. Unlike the national anthem, there are no rules or decorum to be observed when reciting *Vande Mataram*.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Composition")]

[Bankim Chandra Chatterjee](/wiki/Bankim_Chandra_Chatterjee "Bankim Chandra Chatterjee") was one of the earliest graduates of the newly established [Calcutta University](/wiki/Calcutta_University "Calcutta University"). After completing his Bachelor of Arts, he joined the [colonial government](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") as a civil servant, becoming a Deputy Collector and later a [Deputy Magistrate](/wiki/Deputy_Magistrate "Deputy Magistrate"). Chattopadhyay was very interested in recent events in Indian and Bengali history, particularly the [Revolt of 1857](/wiki/Revolt_of_1857 "Revolt of 1857") and the previous century's [Sanyasi Rebellion](/wiki/Sanyasi_Rebellion "Sanyasi Rebellion").[[10]](#cite_note-Lipner-12) Around the same time, the administration was trying to promote "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")" as the anthem for Indian subjects, which Indian nationalists disliked. It is generally believed that the concept of "Vande Mataram" came to Chatterjee when he was still a government official, around 1876.[[11]](#cite_note-mustard-13) He wrote "Vande Mataram" at Chinsura ([Chuchura](/wiki/Hugli-Chuchura "Hugli-Chuchura")), in a white colour house of Adhya Family near [Hooghly river](/wiki/Hooghly_river "Hooghly river") (near Jora Ghat) in [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal").

The novel Anandmath is noted for pro-British sentiments. In this novel, Chatterjee argues that the British rulers are the savior of India and Indians cannot overcome arrogance without the British rule.[[12]](#cite_note-m795-14)[[13]](#cite_note-k829-15)

Chattopadhyay wrote the poem in a spontaneous session using words from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") and [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"). The poem was published in Chattopadhyay's book *[Anandamath](/wiki/Anandamath "Anandamath")* in 1882, which is set in the events of the Sannyasi Rebellion.[[10]](#cite_note-Lipner-12)[[11]](#cite_note-mustard-13) [Jadunath Bhattacharya](/wiki/Jadunath_Bhattacharya "Jadunath Bhattacharya") was asked to set a tune for this poem just after it was written.[[11]](#cite_note-mustard-13)

### Indian independence movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Indian independence movement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Flag_of_India_1907_%28Nationalists_Flag%29.svg/250px-Flag_of_India_1907_%28Nationalists_Flag%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_India_1907_(Nationalists_Flag).svg)

Flag of India 1907

"Vande Mataram" was one of the most popular songs of protest during the [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"). The colonial government in response banned the book and made the recital of the song in public a crime.[[14]](#cite_note-Chatterji2005p71-16) The colonial government imprisoned many [independence activists](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") for disobeying the order, but workers and general public repeatedly violated the ban many times by gathering together in the presence of colonial officials and singing it.[[14]](#cite_note-Chatterji2005p71-16) Rabindranath Tagore sang "Vande Mataram" in 1896 at the [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta") Congress Session held at Beadon Square. Dakhina Charan Sen sang it five years later in 1901 at another session of the Congress at [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"). Poet Sarala Devi Chaudurani sang the song in the [Benares](/wiki/Benares "Benares") Congress Session in 1905. [Lala Lajpat Rai](/wiki/Lala_Lajpat_Rai "Lala Lajpat Rai") started a journal called "Vande Mataram" from [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore").[[11]](#cite_note-mustard-13) [Hiralal Sen](/wiki/Hiralal_Sen "Hiralal Sen") made India's first political film in 1905 which ended with the chant. [Matangini Hazra](/wiki/Matangini_Hazra "Matangini Hazra")'s last words as she was shot to death by the [Crown police](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") were "Vande Mataram".[[15]](#cite_note-Local-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Portrait_Gandhi.jpg/250px-Portrait_Gandhi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_Gandhi.jpg)

Mahatma Gandhi supported the first two verses of Vande Mataram as a national song.[[16]](#cite_note-gandhi1939vm-18)

On 20 May 1906 in [Barisal](/wiki/Barisal "Barisal") (now [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh")), more than ten thousand people, both [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") and [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), marched together with *Vande Mataram* flags in a landmark nationalist procession.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)

In 1907, [Bhikaiji Cama](/wiki/Bhikaiji_Cama "Bhikaiji Cama") (1861–1936) created the first version of India's national flag (the [Tiranga](/wiki/Tiranga "Tiranga")) in [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), Germany, in 1907. It had "Vande Mataram" written on it in the middle band.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

A book titled *Kranti Geetanjali* published by Arya Printing Press ([Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore")) and Bharatiya Press ([Dehradun](/wiki/Dehradun "Dehradun")) in 1929 contains the first two stanzas of the lyrics on page 11[[20]](#cite_note-22) as *Matra Vandana*, and a ghazal composed by Bismil was also given on its back, i.e. page 12.[[21]](#cite_note-23) The book written by the famous [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") of [Kakori](/wiki/Kakori "Kakori") Pandit [Ram Prasad Bismil](/wiki/Ram_Prasad_Bismil "Ram Prasad Bismil") was proscribed by the colonial government.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Mahatama Gandhi supported adoption and the singing of the Vande Mataram song. In January 1946, in a speech given in [Guwahati](/wiki/Guwahati "Guwahati") ([Assam](/wiki/Colonial_Assam "Colonial Assam")), he urged that "Jai Hind should not replace Vande-mataram". He reminded everyone present that Vande-mataram was being sung since the inception of the Congress. He supported the "Jai Hind" greeting, but remanded that this greeting should not be to the exclusion of Vande Mataram. Gandhi was concerned that those who discarded Vande Mataram given the tradition of sacrifice behind it, one day would discard "Jai Hind" also.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[note 1]](#cite_note-26)

### Adoption as national song of India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Adoption as national song of India")]

Parts of the "Vande Mataram" was chosen as the national song in 1937 by the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") as it pursued the independence of India from colonial rule, after a committee consisting of [Maulana Azad](/wiki/Maulana_Azad "Maulana Azad"), [Jawaharlal Nehru](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru "Jawaharlal Nehru"), [Subhash Chandra Bose](/wiki/Subhash_Chandra_Bose "Subhash Chandra Bose"), Acharya Deva, and [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") recommended the adoption.[[24]](#cite_note-noorani1039-27) The entire song was not selected by Hindu leaders in order to respect the sentiments of non-Hindus, and the gathering agreed that anyone should be free to sing an alternate "unobjectionable song" at a national gathering if they do not want to sing Vande Mataram because they find it "objectionable" for a personal reason.[[24]](#cite_note-noorani1039-27) According to the gathered leaders, including the Nobel Laureate Rabindranath Tagore, through the first two stanzas began with an unexceptionable evocation of the beauty of the motherland, in later stanzas there are references to the Hindu goddess [Durga](/wiki/Durga "Durga"). Tagore believed Vande Matram could not unite all communities.[[25]](#cite_note-p314-28) The [All-India Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League") and [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") completely opposed the song in 1937.

In 1937, Indian National Congress President [Subhas Chandra Bose](/wiki/Subhas_Chandra_Bose "Subhas Chandra Bose") asked Tagore for his views on Vande Matram.[[25]](#cite_note-p314-28)[[26]](#cite_note-h135-29) Tagore replied:

> The core of Vande Mataram is a hymn to goddess Durga: this is so plain that there can be no debate about it. Of course Bankim Chandra does show Durga to be inseparably united with Bengal in the end, but no Mussulman can be expected patriotically to worship the ten-handed deity as 'Swadesh' [our native land]. . . . The novel Anandamath is a work of literature, and so the song is appropriate in it. But Parliament is a place of union for all religious groups, and there the song cannot be appropriate.

Thereafter, with the support of [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") and Jawaharlal Nehru, the Indian National Congress decided to adopt only the first two stanzas as the national song to be sung at public gatherings, and other verses that included references to Durga and [Lakshmi](/wiki/Lakshmi "Lakshmi") were removed.[[16]](#cite_note-gandhi1939vm-18)[[27]](#cite_note-30)

[Rajendra Prasad](/wiki/Rajendra_Prasad "Rajendra Prasad"), who was presiding the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly "Constituent Assembly") on 24 January 1950, made the following statement which was also adopted as the final decision on the issue:

> ...The composition consisting of the words and music known as Jana Gana Mana is the National Anthem of India, subject to such alterations in the words as the Government may authorise as occasion arises; and the song Vande Mataram, which has played a historic part in the struggle for Indian freedom, shall be honoured equally with Jana Gana Mana and shall have equal status with it. (Applause). I hope this will satisfy the Members.
>
> :   —*Constituent Assembly of India*, Vol. XII, 24-1-1950[[28]](#cite_note-31)

### Identification with Mother India and Mother Bengal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Identification with Mother India and Mother Bengal")]

While the poem is an ode to the motherland, and the text does not explicitly name the figure, the question of its intended subject is debated. Even though some argue that, the poem initially refers to [Mother Bengal](/wiki/Banga_Mata "Banga Mata")[[29]](#cite_note-ma1-32)[[30]](#cite_note-ma2-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34)[[32]](#cite_note-ma3-35) a view supported by Sri Aurobindo, who referred to it as the "National Anthem of Bengal,"[[33]](#cite_note-36) conversely, a more substantial number of bipartisan political parties, historians and national sources emphasize the poem's broader significance as a pan-Indian symbol: it was officially adopted by the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress") (INC) for all-India use in 1905 and again by the Congress Working Committee in 1937 when it was sung by the [Nobel laureate](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates "List of Nobel laureates") [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") to serve as a militant salutation to [Bharat Mata](/wiki/Bharat_Mata "Bharat Mata") (Mother India), a concept integral to the pan-Indian [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") against the colonial [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[6]](#cite_note-PIB-Anthem1-8)[[34]](#cite_note-CWC-1937-37) Its status as a symbol of the larger national resistance culminated in the Constituent Assembly granting it official status as the National Song in 1950, to be honoured equally with the National Anthem.[[35]](#cite_note-National_Portal-38)[[36]](#cite_note-Britannica-VM-39) Adopted by the Constituent Assembly of India on 24 January 1950, Vande Mataram is the national song of the Republic of India, sharing equal honour with the national anthem, [Jana Gana Mana](/wiki/Jana_Gana_Mana "Jana Gana Mana").[[37]](#cite_note-rh1-40) This status was initially affirmed by President Rajendra Prasad,[[37]](#cite_note-rh1-40) and reaffirmed by the [Madras High Court](/wiki/Madras_High_Court "Madras High Court") in 2017,[[38]](#cite_note-rh2-41) and the [Bhartiya Janta Party](/wiki/Bhartiya_Janta_Party "Bhartiya Janta Party")-ruled [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") in November 2022, despite the Constitution of India containing no explicit reference to a "national song".[[39]](#cite_note-rh3-42)

The poem played a vital role in the [Indian independence movement](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement"). It first gained political significance when it was recited by [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") at Congress in 1896.[[40]](#cite_note-National_Song_of_India-43)[[41]](#cite_note-:0-44)[[42]](#cite_note-Eck2012p95-45) By 1905, it had become popular amongst political activists and freedom fighters as a marching song.[[42]](#cite_note-Eck2012p95-45) The first two verses of the poem were adopted as the National Song of India in October 1937 by the [Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress").[[16]](#cite_note-gandhi1939vm-18)[[43]](#cite_note-46)[[44]](#cite_note-47) The song, as well as *Anandmath*, were banned under [British colonial rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") under threat of imprisonment, making its use revolutionary. The ban was ultimately overturned by the [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") upon [independence](/wiki/Indian_Independence_Act_1947 "Indian Independence Act 1947") in 1947.[[14]](#cite_note-Chatterji2005p71-16)[[45]](#cite_note-48)

## Official status as the National Song

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Official status as the National Song")]

On 24 January 1950, the [Constituent Assembly of India](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_India "Constituent Assembly of India") adopted "Vande Mataram" as the national song of the Republic of India. [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") [Rajendra Prasad](/wiki/Rajendra_Prasad "Rajendra Prasad") stated that the song should be honoured equally with the national anthem of India, "[Jana Gana Mana](/wiki/Jana_Gana_Mana "Jana Gana Mana")".[[37]](#cite_note-rh1-40)

In July 2017, the Madras High Court ruled that the "Vande Mataram" shall be sung or played at least once a week in all schools, universities and other educational institutions of Tamil Nadu. The Court also ruled that the song should be played or sung in government offices and industrial facilities at least once a month.[[38]](#cite_note-rh2-41) However, in November 2017, the Madras High Court backtracked and left the decision for the Tamil Nadu's government to make the decision.[[46]](#cite_note-u216-49)

In November 2022, the [Government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") filed an *[affidavit](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit")* at the [Delhi High Court](/wiki/Delhi_High_Court "Delhi High Court"), stating that "Jana Gana Mana" and "Vande Mataram" would "stand on the same level" and should be accorded equal respect by citizens,[[39]](#cite_note-rh3-42) even though the Constitution of India does not make an explicit reference to a "national song".[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)

> Thou art Goddess Kamala ([Lakshmi](/wiki/Lakshmi "Lakshmi")), [lotus-throned](/wiki/Lotus_throne "Lotus throne"),  
> And Goddess Vani ([Saraswati](/wiki/Saraswati "Saraswati")), bestower of wisdom known  
> Pure and perfect without peer,  
> Mother lend thine ear,  
> Rich with thy hurrying streams,  
> Bright with thy orchard gleams,  
> Dark of hue O candid-fair [Verse 4]  
>   
> In thy soul, with jewelled hair  
> And thy glorious smile divine,  
> Loveliest of all earthly lands,  
> Showering wealth from well-stored hands!  
> Mother, mother mine!  
> Mother sweet, I bow to thee,  
> Mother great and free! [Verse 5]

Apart from the above [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") translation, [Sri Aurobindo](/wiki/Sri_Aurobindo "Sri Aurobindo") also translated "Vande Mataram" into a [verse](/wiki/Verse_(poetry) "Verse (poetry)") form known as "Mother, I Praise Thee".[[53]](#cite_note-sanyal-58) [Sri Aurobindo](/wiki/Sri_Aurobindo "Sri Aurobindo") commented on his English translation of the poem that "[it] is difficult to translate the National Song of India into verse in another language owing to its unique union of sweetness, simple directness and high poetic force."[[54]](#cite_note-59)

### Translation into other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Translation into other languages")]

"Vande Mataram" has inspired many Indian poets and has been translated into numerous Indian languages, such as Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Odia, Malayalam, Assamese, Hindi, Marathi, Gujarati, Punjabi, Urdu, and others.[[55]](#cite_note-60)[[note 2]](#cite_note-62)

[Arif Mohammad Khan](/wiki/Arif_Mohammad_Khan "Arif Mohammad Khan") translated "Vande Mataram" into [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu").[[57]](#cite_note-63) It can be read in Urdu ([Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") script) as:

## Ongoing demand for national anthem status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ongoing demand for national anthem status")]

Despite the Constituent Assembly of India declaring "Vande Mataram" as the "National Song of India" with "equal honour" to the National Anthem of India, "Jana Gana Mana", an intermittent political and legal demands persists regarding the elevating the status of Vande Mataram to the national anthem.[[36]](#cite_note-Britannica-VM-39) Advocates for elevating Vande Mataram to the status of national anthem often cite its pivotal role as the rallying cry of the Indian independence movement, arguing that its historical significance and revolutionary background surpass that of the current anthem.[[6]](#cite_note-PIB-Anthem1-8) This demand has led to several [PIL](/wiki/Public_interest_litigation "Public interest litigation") pleas in various courts, including the [Delhi High Court](/wiki/Delhi_High_Court "Delhi High Court"), seeking a directive to grant it equal status to, or replace, "Jana Gana Mana"; however, such petitions have historically been dismissed, with courts noting that the decision is not a matter for judicial interference.[[59]](#cite_note-65) But, these persistent demands continue to resurface.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vande_Mataram&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The poem has been set to a large number of tunes. The oldest surviving audio recordings date to 1907, and there have been more than a hundred different versions recorded throughout the 20th century. Many of these versions have employed traditional [Indian](/wiki/India "India") [classical](/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music "Hindustani classical music") [ragas](/wiki/Raga "Raga"). Versions of the song have been visualised on celluloid in a number of films, including *[Leader](/wiki/Leader_(1964_film) "Leader (1964 film)")*, *[Amar Asha](/wiki/Amar_Asha "Amar Asha")*, and *[Anand Math](/wiki/Anand_Math "Anand Math")*. It is widely believed that the tune set for [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio") station version was composed by [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar").[[11]](#cite_note-mustard-13) [Hemant Kumar](/wiki/Hemant_Kumar "Hemant Kumar") composed music for the song in the movie [Anand Math](/wiki/Anand_Math "Anand Math") in 1952. Many singers like [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar"), [K.S.Chithra](/wiki/K.S.Chithra "K.S.Chithra") sung made it cult classic.[[61]](#cite_note-red-67) In 2002, [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service") conducted an international poll to choose ten most famous songs of all time. Around 7000 songs were selected from all over the world. "Vande Mataram", from the movie *[Anand Math](/wiki/Anand_Math "Anand Math")*, was ranked second.[[62]](#cite_note-68) All India Radio's version and some other versions are in [Desh](/wiki/Desh_(raga) "Desh (raga)") [raga](/wiki/Raga "Raga").[[63]](#cite_note-69)

* [Vande Mataram (album)](/wiki/Vande_Mataram_(album) "Vande Mataram (album)"), 1997 studio album by Indian musician [A. R. Rehman](/wiki/A._R._Rehman "A. R. Rehman").

* [Vandae Maatharam](/wiki/Vandae_Maatharam "Vandae Maatharam"), 2010 Indian action film in Malyalam and Tamil featuring [Mammootty](/wiki/Mammootty "Mammootty") and [Arjun Sarja](/wiki/Arjun_Sarja "Arjun Sarja")
